This is in response to your letter of July 7, 1980, 
requesting advice on behalf of Brent Ventura concerning the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
The following advlJe is provided pur uant to Government Code 
Section 83l14(b).-
The facts as I understand them from your 
letter and from our telephone conversations are as follows. 
Mr. Ventura is a member of t 
Town Council, (i.e., 
ci 
council) of the Town of Los Gatos. 
He currently ha 
a 
one-sixth beneficial interest in an irrevocable testamentary 
trust, and upon the death of his father one-third (two-sixths) 
of the corpus will be distributed to Mr. Ventura. 
One of 
two major assets of the trust is a seven-unit apartment 
building in Los Gatos worth approximately $300,000 to $4 0,000 
which grosses about $1,400 to $1,600 per month in rents 
The present value of one-sixth of the corpus of the trust is 
between $10,000 and $100,000. 
At its next meeting the Town 
Council will discuss rent control, mediation and arbitration 
matters, and you hav 
asked the 
xtent to which Mr. Ventura 
may participa 
in those discussions as a membe 
of the Town 
Council given his interest in the trust • 
. !/ All statutory refe 
nces are to the Government Code 
unless ot 
rwise noted. 


Mr. Pre s to n H i 11 
July 17, 1980 
Two 
Section 87100 of the Political Reform Ac 
provides that 
no public official 
make, participate in making or use 
his official position to influence a decision in which he 
has a financial interest, and Section 87103 provides that: 
An official has a financial interest in 
decision 
within the meaning of Sect 
n 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial ef ct, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(b) Any real pr 
rty in which the public official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one 
thousand dollars ($1,000), 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned 
by ••• a trust in which [the public official] has a 
substantial interest. • . • 
A public official has a 
s 
tantial interest in a trust when the official, his 
spouse and dependent children have a present or future 
interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
The definit~on of the term "interest in rea 
property" 
as it is used in the Act provides, inter alia, that "In-
terests in real property of an individual includes a pro 
rata share of interests in real property of any. 
• trust 
in which the individual • . • owns directly, indirectly or 
beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater. 
" 
Section 82033. 
Pursuant to regulations of the Commission, a 
public official has a direct, indirect or beneficial in-
terest in a trust if he is a beneficiary and has an ir-
revocable future right to receive income or principal. 
See 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18234(c)(2)(B). 
Although the 
tence of Section 87103 and the 
definitional sect 
i 
somewhat dif 
rent standards 
(a problem we are a 
solve this 
ar through 
legis ation}, it is cle r that by operation of either the 10 
percent rule contained in s ction 82033 or the "substantial 
interest" provision 0 
S 
7103 itse ff Mr. Ventura 
does have an i terest 
rent 
r 
held by the 
trust. 
Co 
uently, 
must not make, partici 
te in making 


Mr. Preston Hill 
July 17, 1980 
Page Three 
or otherwise use his official position to. influence a decision 
of the Town Council which could have a material financial 
effect on the property which would be distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. 
In the Ferraro opinion, 
4 FPPC Ops. 62 (No. 78-009, 1978), the Commission aecided 
that the effect of a rent control decision on a person who 
owns four or more units of rental property is in fact dis-
tinguishable from the effect the decision will have on the 
public generally, and in the Gillmor opinion, 3 FPPC Ops. 38 
(No. 76-089, 1977), the Commission determined that if a 
government action is intended to have a certain effect, such 
an effect is "foreseeable." 
As rent control measures are 
d signed to limit the amount of income which can be earned 
from rental property, it is therefore clearly foreseeable 
that a rent control measure will have a financial effect on 
the rental property in which Mr. Ventura has an interest. 
The remaining question is therefore whether such an 
effect would be "material" within the meaning of Section 
87103. 
By regulation the Commission has provided 
hat: 
(a) 
The financial effect of a governmental decision 
on a financial interest of a public official is material 
if the decision will have a s 
nificant effect on the 
business entity, real property or source of income in 
question. 
(b) 
In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision 
will be significant within the meaning of a general 
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration 
should be given to the following factors: 
(2) 
Whether, in the case of a direct or 
indirect interest in real property of one thousand 
dollars or more held by a public Official, the 
effect of the decision will be to increase or 
decrease: 
(A) 
The income producing potential of 
the property by the lesser of: 
1. 
One thousand dollars ($1,000) 
r month; or 


Mr. Preston Hill 
July 17, 1980 
Page Four 
2. 
Five percent per month if it is 
fifty dollars ($50) or more per month; 
or 
(n) 
The fair market value of the property 
by the lesser of: 
1. 
Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); 
or 
2. 
One half of one percent if it 
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or 
more. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 
Applying these stan<lards to the property in which Mr. Ventura 
has an interest, the effect of a rent control ordinance 
could be considered significant if it would decrease the 
amount of rent which could otherwise be received from the 
ilding by from $70 to $90 per month (5 percent of $1,400 
to $1,600), or if it would decrease the fair market value of 
the property by from $1,500 to $2,000 (one-half of one percent 
of $300,000 to $400,000). 
As the building has seven units, 
any ordinance which created a differential of from $10 to 
$15 per month between the amount of rent which was allowable 
under 
ordinance and the amount of rent which could other-
wise be charged per unit would have a material financial 
effect on the property. 
Given these amounts, it is obvious that it does not 
r 
uire a very stringent rent control measure in order to 
create a material financi 1 effect on rental property. 
If 
there were a specific proposal which would clearly have such 
a minor effect as to fail to meet these standards, an of-
ficial who had an interest in rental property would not be 
uired to disqualify himself from participating in it. 
However it is only reasonab 
to assume (in the absence of 
such a specific proposal) that the Town Council's discussion 
of rent control will lead to consideration of a measure or 
alternative measures which would have a material financial 
ef ct on rental 
rty. -Consequently, Mr. Ventura should 
not participate in the Town Council's decisio 
regarding 
rent control ~ 
see 


Mr. Preston Hill 
July 17, 1980 
Page Five 
with respect to ~ediation and arbitration, if those 
questions 
re 
tertwined with the concept of rent control, 
e.g., if 
iation or arbitration system will decide how 
much ren 
ca 
be cha 
for a particular unit under a defined 
set of circumstances, then the Town Council's decision on 
those questions could also foresee 
ly affect the amount of 
rent which could be collected from the building in which 
Mr. Ventura has an interest, and he should disqualify him-
self from those decisions as well. 
If the concept of rent 
control were to be rejected by the Town Council without 
Mr. Ventura's participation, however, and the question of 
mediation and arbitration of other kinds of landlord-tenant 
problems were to be raised, our advice concerning his par-
ticipation in such decisions might be different. 
If such 
turns out to be the case, please feel free to contact us 
aga

ur of our

increase in order for it to be considered material. 
You 
and Mr. Ventura will be able to make such calculations 
based upon the reasonably foreseeable effects of the 
decision to re-adopt or not to re-adopt. 
We trust that this advice is responsive to you and

Should you have further questions, 
please do not hesitate to contact us. 
I may be reached at 
916/322-5901.

Re: 
Brent Ventura; Follow-Up on Advice Letter No. 82-063 
Dear l1r. Leidigh: 
Thank you very much for your very helpful letter of April 20, 1982. 
l1r. Ventura has asked me to request an additional opinion on a closely related 
subject; namely, whether he may participate in a re-adoption of Chapter 24 
when it expires. 
I infonned l1r. Ventura that in my opinion, the answer was clearly implied by the 
analysis in yrur letter of April 20, 1982. 
However, since he is lmder considerable 
pressure to vote, if possible, he has asked that I obtain a fonnal opinion. 
You 
need not repeat the detailed analysis in the earlier letter, but can simply in-
corporate it by reference. 
Thank you again for your assistance.

July 7, 1980 
Ms. Sarah Cameron 
Town Attorney 
354-6880 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street Building 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Ms. Cameron: 
This letter is a follow-up to our telephone conver-
sation; a member of our Town council needs written advice, 
either in the form of an opinion or an informal letter, 
on a question of whether he is qualified to participate 
in the discussion of, and to vote on, residential rent 
control, mediation, and arbi tration matters. 
(The word 
"Town" is part of Los Gatos' name. 
The Town is a general 
law city with the usual five-member city council govern-
ment.) 
The council meeting at which these matters will 
be taken up will be held in two weeks. 
I cannot give a 
detailed description of the ordinance or resolution that 
would be before the council. 
It must suffice to say that 
any form of regulation or assistance in resolving disputes 
may be considered, but that seven-unit buildings would not 
be exempted from any measures adopted. 
The counci1member who asked the question is a one-sixth 
beneficiary of a testamentary trust. 
The value of a one-
sixth of the corpus is in the $10,000-$100,000 range. 
On the death of the councilmember's father, who is 53 years 
old and in good health, two-sixths of the corpus will be 
distributed to the counci1member. 
The trust is not re-
vocable. 
The councilmember has never received income 
distributions from the trust, and assumes that the instru-
ment or trust requires none. 
The trust has two major assets, a drugstore business 
operated in leased premises and a seven-unit apartment 
house worth roughly $300,000-$400,000. 
The operation of 
the apartment house grosses around $1,400-$1,600 monthly. 
The counci1member does not know the net return. 
CENTER 
fp 
110 EAST MAIN STREET 
'Ill 
p.o. BOX 949 • 
LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 


Ms. Sarah Cameron 
-2-
July 7, 1980 
Judging from the discussion you and I had earlier, 
this letter contains more information than is necessary 
for you to reach a conclusion, but I prefer to err in the 
direction of giving too many facts rather than too few. 
ruly yours, 
Town Attorney

sections.conclusion: Mr. Ventura should not participate in the Town Council's decision regarding rent control. With respect to mediation and arbitration, if those questions are intertwined with the concept of rent control (e.g., if a mediation or arbitration system will decide how much rent can be charged), then Mr. Ventura should also disqualify himself from those decisions. However, if the concept of rent control were rejected and mediation and arbitration of other kinds of landlord-tenant problems were raised separately, different advice might apply.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Town Council member with a beneficial interest in a trust holding rental property can participate in Council discussions and decisions regarding rent control, mediation, and arbitration measures.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Ventura has a disqualifying financial interest in the rental property held by the trust and must disqualify himself from participating in rent control decisions because such measures would foreseeably have a material financial effect on the property. His disqualification regarding mediation and arbitration depends on whether those matters are intertwined with rent control.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document includes OCR artifacts and mixed formatting. Primary advice letter (No. 80-090) dated July 17, 1980, with two follow-up documents included: incoming request letter of July 7, 1980, and FPPC response letter dated May 13, 1982 (No. 82-083) incorporating the original analysis by reference. Extracted data primarily from the main advice letter sections.

rent which could be collected from the building in which 
Mr. Ventura has an interest, and he should disqualify him-
self from those decisions as well. 
If the concept of rent 
control were to be rejected by the Town Council without 
Mr. Ventura's participation, however, and the question of 
mediation and arbitration of other kinds of landlord-tenant 
problems were to be raised, our advice concerning his par-
ticipation in such decisions might be different. 
If such 
turns out to be the case, please feel free to contact us

We trust that this advice is responsive to you and 
Mr. Ventura's request.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Ventura should not participate in the Town Council's decision regarding rent control. With respect to mediation and arbitration, if those questions are intertwined with the concept of rent control (e.g., if a mediation or arbitration system will decide how much rent can be charged), then Mr. Ventura should also disqualify himself from those decisions. However, if the concept of rent control were rejected and mediation and arbitration of other kinds of landlord-tenant problems were raised separately, different advice might apply.

Box 949 Los Gatos, CA 95030 Dear Mr. Hill: Adminiitrotion •• Executive/101lal • '. Enforcement 322-5660 322-5901 322-6.«1 July 17, 1980 7-(J90. Sfatements of Economic Int.re.t 322-6444 This is in response to your letter of July 7, 1980, requesting advice on behalf of Brent Ventura concerning the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The following advlJe is provided pur uant to Government Code Section 83l14(b).- The facts as I understand them from your letter and from our telephone conversations are as follows. Mr. Ventura is a member of t Town Council, (i.e., ci council) of the Town of Los Gatos. He currently ha a one-sixth beneficial interest in an irrevocable testamentary trust, and upon the death of his father one-third (two-sixths) of the corpus will be distributed to Mr. Ventura. One of two major assets of the trust is a seven-unit apartment building in Los Gatos worth approximately $300,000 to $4 0,000 which grosses about $1,400 to $1,600 per month in rents The present value of one-sixth of the corpus of the trust is between $10,000 and $100,000. At its next meeting the Town Council will discuss rent control, mediation and arbitration matters, and you hav asked the xtent to which Mr. Ventura may participa in those discussions as a membe of the Town Council given his interest in the trust • . !/ All statutory refe nces are to the Government Code unless ot rwise noted. Mr. Pre s to n H i 11 July 17, 1980 Two Section 87100 of the Political Reform Ac provides that no public official make, participate in making or use his official position to influence a decision in which he has a financial interest, and Section 87103 provides that: An official has a financial interest in decision within the meaning of Sect n 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef ct, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (b) Any real pr rty in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000), For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by ••• a trust in which [the public official] has a substantial interest. • . • A public official has a s tantial interest in a trust when the official, his spouse and dependent children have a present or future interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). The definit~on of the term "interest in rea property" as it is used in the Act provides, inter alia, that "In- terests in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any. • trust in which the individual • . • owns directly, indirectly or
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter on conflict of interest for Town Council member Brent Ventura regarding his ability to participate in discussions of rent control and related matters, given his beneficial interest in a trust holding rental property. The letter concludes he must disqualify himself from rent control decisions due to the material financial effect such measures would have on his property interest.
